ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Kerabu Daging exists; mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_03301710) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific Malay salad term is not currently present in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the primary ingredient anchor for this mixed beef salad preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 composite code exists for Kerabu Daging as a prepared dish. A0CKP (cooked beef) is used as the closest parent ingredient anchor. The composite salad nature of the dish is documented in notes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kerabu Daging or equivalent Malay beef salad preparation.

main_ingredients: kerisik (toasted grated coconut) or beras sangrai (toasted ground rice)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kerabu Daging is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate side-dish portion as typically served alongside white rice in a Malaysian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Malaysian Foods — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (closest analogous composite beef salad entry)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Kerabu Daging entry was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Singapore FCD as a single composite record. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data for boiled lean beef, toasted coconut (kerisik), lime juice, shallots, lemongrass, and bean sprouts using IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore FCD as primary references, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this component-level estimation and have been retained with source attribution updated to reflect the approved source basis.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate mixed beef salad with toasted rice and vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kerabu Daging exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate mixed meat salads with small amounts of toasted rice (beras sangrai) and vegetables. The high protein content (14.5 g/100 g), moderate fat (8.2 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) substantially attenuate the glycemic response of the 9.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 9.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.0 g, classified as low. This estimation is consistent with the input record's GI of 35 (confidence 0.7, assessment_type: estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg/100 g) from fish sauce and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced fish sauce or salt in the dressing, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 480 mg sodium (~21% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); the dish's potassium content (280 mg/100 g) partially offsets sodium's pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and high protein content (14.5 g/100 g) supporting glycemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kerabu Daging is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~35), high protein (14.5 g/100 g), and moderate fat content, which collectively slow glucose absorption; a standard 150 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 5 g, well within the low-GL threshold, though added sugar in the dressing should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and kerisik (toasted coconut) and sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean cuts of beef and limit kerisik quantity to reduce saturated fat intake (estimated 3.1 g/100 g), and request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium; the dish's potassium, zinc, and B-vitamin content are beneficial, but total saturated fat and sodium intake should be monitored within daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (lemongrass, bunga kantan) balanced against potential red meat and chili triggers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kerabu Daging contains anti-inflammatory aromatics including lemongrass and ginger torch flower; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to red meat or nightshade-family foods (bird's eye chilies) should monitor personal tolerance and consider reducing chili quantity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kerabu Daging is a traditional Malay spicy beef salad originating from the northern states of Malaysia, particularly Kelantan and Terengganu. The dish is prepared by briefly boiling or poaching thin slices of beef until just tender, then tossing them in a vibrant dressing of fresh lime juice, fish sauce or salt, and a small amount of sugar. The dressing is enriched with finely sliced aromatics including shallots, lemongrass, bunga kantan (ginger torch flower), and bird's eye chilies, which give the salad its characteristic zingy, spicy, and fragrant profile. A defining textural and flavour element is kerisik (toasted grated coconut) or beras sangrai (toasted ground rice), which imparts a nutty depth and a pleasantly gritty mouthfeel. Fresh vegetables such as bean sprouts or winged beans are commonly added to bulk out the salad and add crunch. The result is a refreshing, protein-rich dish with a complex interplay of sour, spicy, savoury, and nutty flavours. Nutritionally, Kerabu Daging provides approximately 165 kcal per 100 g, with 14.5 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 9.5 g carbohydrate. Its low glycemic index (~35) and high protein content make it suitable for blood sugar management. Sodium content (320 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is traditionally served as a side dish or appetizer alongside white rice at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact Kerabu Daging composite entry was found in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore FCD. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data for boiled lean beef, toasted coconut, lime juice, shallots, lemongrass, and bean sprouts using approved sources (IMR MyFCD, HPB Singapore FCD, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy). The AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement available). Confidence is therefore set to medium.
notes: 1. No exact Kerabu Daging composite record was found in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore FCD; nutrient values are component-level estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients (boiled lean beef, toasted coconut, lime juice, shallots, lemongrass, bean sprouts). 2. saturated_fat_g (3.1 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.0 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for boiled lean beef and toasted coconut (kerisik) proportions typical of this dish; these values are not directly reported in a single approved source for this composite dish. 3. cholesterol_mg and water_g are set to -1: detailed cholesterol and moisture data for this specific composite Malay beef salad preparation are not available as a single entry in IMR MyFCD, HPB Singapore FCD, or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate mixed meat salads with small amounts of toasted rice; no direct GI measurement for Kerabu Daging exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 9.5 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 5.0 g (low). 5. FoodOn mapped to closest parent 'beef dish' class (FOODON_03301710); no exact Malay salad term exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (cooked beef) used as closest parent ingredient anchor; no composite FoodEx2 code for Kerabu Daging exists. Both ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values in VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md. 6. This is a Malaysian dish (Malay cuisine); G6 multilingual rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Malay local name is present. Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable for this Malaysian Malay dish. 7. The input source citation ('Nutrient Composition of Traditional Malaysian Salads (Kerabu)', HPB Singapore, https://www.hpb.gov.sg) could not be verified as a specific published HPB document; it has been retained as a supporting reference but the nutrient_source has been updated to reflect the broader approved source basis.
